summary: USA Pickleball does not publish an approved-net product list. Nets are governed by a written specification in the rulebook (Section 2.B). Any net meeting these dimensions is acceptable for sanctioned play.

specs.label: Total length (post-to-post)
specs.note: Measured between the inside of the posts.

specs.label: Court width covered
specs.note: Court is 20 ft wide; net extends 1 ft beyond each sideline (post setback).

specs.label: Net height at the sidelines (posts)
specs.note: Top of the net at the posts.

specs.label: Net height at the center
specs.note: The net sags 2 inches at center; a center strap pulls it down to spec.

specs.label: Mesh size
specs.value: Small enough that the ball cannot pass through
specs.note: No specific weave or mesh hole-size dimension is mandated.

specs.label: Top tape / cord
specs.value: Required — must run the full length
specs.note: A 2-inch white binding along the top edge is standard.

specs.label: Center strap
specs.note: Pulls the net down to the 34-inch center height. Adjustable on most regulation-grade nets.

specs.label: Post diameter
specs.note: Posts must not interfere with play. Round or oval cross-section.

buyerNotes.label: Portable vs permanent
buyerNotes.text: Sanctioned tournaments use permanent in-ground nets. Permanent nets hold tension better and meet the 36/34-inch heights consistently. Portable nets vary widely in tension; cheap portable nets often sag below regulation when warm.

buyerNotes.label: What to look for
buyerNotes.text: (1) Adjustable center strap, (2) regulation 22-ft length, (3) tensioning system that won't loosen mid-match, (4) sturdy posts that don't bow under tension.

buyerNotes.label: What to avoid
buyerNotes.text: Tennis nets cut down (height is wrong), badminton nets (lighter mesh fails repeatedly under pickleball impact), and nets without a center strap (they will sag below 34 inches at the middle).
